coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanozyme based dual-channel lipopolysaccharide sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Limin
Zhang, Cai
Shan, Shuo
Zhang, Yifan
Chen, Shengnan
Wu, Tianming
Niu, Yusheng
Xu, Yuanhong
coredata.dc:description: The release of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) during the proliferation and death of bacterial growth could cause acute versatile disorders like anaphylaxis and multiple organ failure. Analysis methods with high sensitivity and selectivity are expected to be developed to replace the limuluslysatetest. Classical colorimetric method was equipped with portable visualization but limited with low sensitivity, while incredibly sensitive fluorescence sensing strategy suffered from negligible selectivity. Delicate design of the dual channels analysis would make the ends of such dilemma based on a fluorescent nanozyme of iron doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) with both excellent fluorescence and peroxidase-mimic catalytic activity. Accordingly, a complementary dual channel analytical platform of LPS was prepared. The excellent peroxidase-like activity imparted the dominant selectivity for LPS (available under 6 interferences), while the powerful fluorescence property endowed the excellent sensitivity with the limit of detection (LOD) of an unprecedented level (0.0023 EU/mL). Our study has broadened a new path for design of efficient analytical methods via complementway.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LPS
Colorimetry
Fluorescence
CQDs

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-mimic catalytic activity and kinetics study of Fe-C QDs
Experimental proof of hydroxyl radical (•OH) production
Sensing the LPS with colorimetry method
Sensing the LPS with fluorescence platform
Construction of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B
Fluorescence characterization of Fe-C QDs and Fe-C QDs@Poly B
Investigation of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-C QDs
Sensing of the LPS by dual channels system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological characterization of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs. (a) The TEM image of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs. (b) The HRTEM image of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs. (c) The XRD of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs. (d) The FTIR of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs.
High-resolution XPS characterizations. (a) The XPS survey of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs. (b) O 1 s, (c) C 1 s and (d) Fe 2p spectra of the as-prepared Fe-C QDs.

Construction of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B (a) Zeta potential changes of Fe-C QDs before and after Poly B modification. (b) Changes of hydration particle size of Fe-C QDs before and after Pm B modification. (c) EIS characterizations, in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- contained 100 mM KCl solution. (d) CVs of solution of 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- contained 100 mM KCl at the scan rate of 100 mV/s.

Fluorescence properties of the Fe-C QDs and Fe-C QDs@Poly B. (a) Optimal emission (EM) and excitation (EX) in Fluorescence spectra. (b) Fluorescence spectra at gradually incremental excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 390 nm. (c) CIE 1931 chromaticity chart of the Fe-C QDs. (d) EX, EM changed of Fe-C QDs before and after Poly B modification. (e) Fluorescence spectra at gradually incremental excitation wavelengths ranging from 370 nm to 430 nm. (f) CIE 1931 chromaticity chart of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B.

The study of POD-like activity. (a) The absorbance spectra of different reaction systems: TMB + H2O2, Fe-C QDs + H2O2, Fe-C QDs + TMB, Fe-C QDs + TMB + H2O2. (b) POD-like activity changed of Fe-C QDs before and after Poly B modification. (c) Fluorescence spectra of a TA-containing solution include only TA, TA + H2O2, TA + Fe-C QDs and TA + H2O2 + Fe-C QDs. (d) Fluorescence spectra changes of Fe-C QDs before and after Poly B modification. (e) and (f) Temperature and pH dependences of the POD-like activity of Fe-C QDs. The error bars stand for the standard deviation (n = 3).

Steady-state kinetic assay of Fe-C QDs. (a) The concentration of TMB was changed while the H2O2 was fixed at 1 mM. (b) The concentration of H2O2 was changed while the TMB was fixed at 2 mM. (c) and (d) Double-reciprocal plots for the POD-like activity of Fe-C QDs. The error bars stand for the standard deviation (n = 3).

Dual channels analysis of LPS. (a) Absorbance changes of Fe-C QDs@Poly B with the increasement of LPS. (b) Linear ship and regression equation of LPS sensing by colorimetric (ΔAbsorbance means the difference of absorbance between experimental and blank groups). (c) Selectivity of Fe-C QDs@Poly B to LPS (I: hyaluronic acid, II: β-renieratene, III: arachidic acid, IV: jasmonic acid, V: phosphatidycholine, VI: chitosan, VII: LPS, VIII: I-VII). (d) The fluorescence intensity quenching image with the increasement of LPS concentration. (e) Linear ship and regression equation of LPS sensing by fluorescence (△Intensity means the difference of fluorescence intensity within experimental and blank groups). (f) The selectivity of Fe-C QDs@Poly B to LPS by fluorescence platform (I: hyaluronic acid, II:β-renieratene, III: arachidic acid, IV: jasmonic acid, V: phosphatidycholine, VI: chitosan, VII: LPS, VII:I-VII). The error bars represent average data ± SD (n = 3).
Investigation of the mechanism of dual channels system.

Schematic illustration of dual channels detection of LPS based on Fe-C QDs@Poly B. (a) Construction of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B with extraordinary specificity for LPS. (b) Illustration of the dual channels system in analysis of LPS.
Accuracy of the dual channel method compared with commercial kits.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanozyme based dual-channel lipopolysaccharide sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Tourism and Geography Science, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Tourism and Geography Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Tourism and Geography Science, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Hainan Medical University, Haikou 570311, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Hainan Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Hainan Medical University, Haikou 570311, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Children's Hospital Capital Institute of Pediatrics, Beijing 100020, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Children's Hospital Capital Institute of Pediatrics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Children's Hospital Capital Institute of Pediatrics, Beijing 100020, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China (Y. Niu).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L. Li and C. Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of dual channels detection of LPS based on Fe-C QDs@Poly B. (a) Construction of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B with extraordinary specificity for LPS. (b) Illustration of the dual channels system in analysis of LPS.

The release of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) during the proliferation and death of bacterial growth could cause acute versatile disorders like anaphylaxis and multiple organ failure. Analysis methods with high sensitivity and selectivity are expected to be developed to replace the limuluslysatetest. Classical colorimetric method was equipped with portable visualization but limited with low sensitivity, while incredibly sensitive fluorescence sensing strategy suffered from negligible selectivity. Delicate design of the dual channels analysis would make the ends of such dilemma based on a fluorescent nanozyme of iron doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) with both excellent fluorescence and peroxidase-mimic catalytic activity. Accordingly, a complementary dual channel analytical platform of LPS was prepared. The excellent peroxidase-like activity imparted the dominant selectivity for LPS (available under 6 interferences), while the powerful fluorescence property endowed the excellent sensitivity with the limit of detection (LOD) of an unprecedented level (0.0023 EU/mL). Our study has broadened a new path for design of efficient analytical methods via complementway.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS), also known as endotoxin, is one of the major components and toxic inflammatory factors of most gram-negative bacterium’s out membrane [1,2]. During the entire life, the proliferation and decomposition of bacteria could intrigue the release of LPS [3,4]. Even at an extreme trace level, LPS still retains high toxicity and activity [5,6]. Therefore, it is urgent necessary to develop sensitive and specific analytical platform for LPS in clinical diagnosis and therapeutics. In general, the standard LPS analytical method on the market was based on limulus reagent cracking liquid (LAL). Although, this analytical technique was comparatively acute for LPS, the shortcomings are obvious such as time-consuming procedures, unaffordable expenditure, unable to ensure the accuracy of result in complex samples [7–9]. During the recent decades, plenty specific ligands both natural and artificial had been developed for endotoxin recognition [10–12], such as antibody [10,13], functionalized polydiacetylene liposomes [11] and pyrene derivatives [12]. Part of them had been put for commercial usage like polymyxin B, which exhibited both high affinity and toxicity neutralizing ability for LPS. Simultaneously, numerous methods had been constructed for LPS detection in the past decades, like surface enhanced Raman spectra (SERS) [14], electrochemistry [15], mass spectra [16], CRISPR-Cas based method [17], etc. However, the dilemma of high expenditure, sophisticated devices, professional experimentalists was still needed.

Colorimetric method was promising for substitution of LAL in LPS analysis due to its facile visualization. Therein, nanozyme-based ones were prominent and promising due to its easy functionalization. The introduction of specific ligand enabled the nanozyme-based colorimetry with better selectivity. However, the high limit of detection and poor sensitivity of most colorimetric detection methods restricted their application. The quantum dots (QDs) based fluorescent detection method was also prevalent with obvious advantages of eco-friendliness, cost-effectiveness and dominant sensitivity. Unfortunately, the unclear selectivity constricted its wider application [18–20]. Hence, fluorescent quantum dots based nanozyme would be a satisfied choice for construction of highly efficient analytical platform due to its inherent property of fluorescent and enzyme-mimicking activity.

Herein, this work has innovated a new path for detection of LPS with high sensitivity and selectivity based on the fluorescent nanozyme of Fe-C QDs with both excellent fluorescence and peroxidase-like properties. They have been produced through one-step solvothermal method based on the precursors of critic acid and FeCl2. Fluorescence and colorimetric dual- channel monitoring platform of LPS have been implemented by the modification of Poly B as recognition ligand on the Fe-C QDs (Fe-C QDs@Poly B). The poly B functionalized peroxidase-like enzyme activity provided dominant colorimetric sensing property for distinguished selectivity. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.0023 EU/mL in the fluorescence sensing system. Based on the dual channels analytical platform, the disadvantaged poor sensitivity of colorimetric method had been complimented with the fluorescence system. At the meantime, indistinguishable selectivity of fluorescence analysis had been averted. Thus, an efficient analytical method of LPS had been constructed by the combination of complemented and imperfect methods, meanwhile, a novel philosophy had been brought for construction of biosensor with high efficiency.
The materials and regents had been described detailly in Supplementary Information.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) was carried out on JEOL JEM-2100 microscope instrument at acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the particles were recorded on Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with copper Kα (λ = 0.154056 nm) radiation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on an ESCALAB 250Xi XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an Al Kα X-ray source. The PL spectra were measured on a FS5-TCSPC-1189-0317-A3732-1 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, United Kingdom). The UV absorption spectra were recorded on a Mapada UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). The absorbance at 450 or 650 nm was measured by an enzyme-labeled instrument (Thermo Scientific Multiskan FC, Shanghai). Electrochemical measurements were all recorded on Parstat 4000A (Princeton, USA). Hydrodynamic size and zeta potentials were recorded on Zetasizer-Lab (Malvern, UK).

Fe-C QDs was prepared via one-step solvothermal method using critic acid as carbon basement, FeCl2 for precursors, and caffeic acid to avoid oxidation. Firstly, 2 g critic acid, 0.01 g caffeic acid and 0.1 g FeCl2·4H2O were mixed in a 5 mL beaker, then, the mixture was heated to 150 ℃. After turned to be transparently orange, the calefaction was halt and transfer into in a muffle furnace then heated to 500 ℃ under air atmosphere for 4 h, and 1 M NaOH was drippy added for further carbonization. After ultrafiltration, the solvent was centrifugated 3 times at 4000 rpm (5 min, room temperature), the Fe-C QDs powder was obtained after dried under vacuum. The usage of iron was optimized from 0.01 g to 0.5 g and the absorbance data of peroxidase activity under versatile usage was recorded. To obtain Poly B modified Fe-C QDs, 200 μL 0.05 g/mL Fe-C QDs was dissolved in 2 mL 0.1 M pH 6.7 2-(N-Morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid monohydrate (MES) at first, then, 400 mg EDC and 800 mg NHS were added for 10 min reaction to activate the carboxyl groups of Fe-C QDs. Finally, 1 mg Poly B was added to the solution and stored at 4 ℃ for 10 h to get the Fe-C QDs@Poly B. The usage of Poly B was also optimized with 0.1 mg to 10 mg and the absorbance data of peroxidase activity under versatile usage was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-mimic catalytic activity and kinetics study of Fe-C QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase-mimic catalytic activity of Fe-C QDs were measured by 20 mM 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as substrate for color rendering with H2O2 (100 mM) in 1 mL acetate buffer solution (100 mM, pH 4.0). The measurement was separated to the four groups, 1: 50 μL 20 mM TMB and 50 μL 100 mM H2O2; 2: 20 μL 0.05 g/mL Fe-C QDs, 50 μL 20 mM TMB and 50 μL 100 mM H2O2; 3: 20 μL 0.05 g/mL Fe-C QDs and 50 μL 20 mM TMB; 4: 20 μL 0.05 g/mL Fe-C QDs and 50 μL 100 mM H2O2. Each group was filled the volume to 1 mL with 100 mM pH 4.0 acetate buffer solution and reacted under room temperature for 10 min. The absorbance data at 652 nm was recorded on the ultraviolet spectrophotometer. After that, the POD-like activity of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B was further researched under same experimental conditions. Subsequently, the optimist pH and temperature condition for the POD-like activity of Fe-C QDs had been explored. The pH dependence of the POD-like activity of the Fe-C QDs was detected in different buffer solutions with pH values ranging from 2.0 to 11.0. The temperature dependence detected at different temperatures increased gradually from 20 to 90 °C. Finally, the catalytic activity of the Fe-C QDs under different concentrations was measured of TMB and H2O2 for kinetics study. The kinetic parameter was obtained by using Michaelis equation calculation method:
V =
V
max
×
[ S ]
K m
+
[ S ]

where Vmax is the maximal reaction speed, [S] is the concentration of substrate, V is the initial velocity and Km is the Michaelis constant. Vmax and Km were obtained by the Line weavere-Burk plot method [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental proof of hydroxyl radical (•OH) production
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the ability of hydroxyl radical (•OH) production by Fe-C QDs, terephthalic acid (TA) had been selected as fluorescence probe to verify the production of •OH. In this classical reacted system, 20 μL 0.05 g/mL Fe-C QDs with 200 μL 0.5 mM TA and 20 μL 1 mM H2O2 was reacted for 12 h during room temperature in 2 mL 0.2 M pH 7.0 phosphate buffer solution. The fluorescence intensity of different system was recorded on fluorescence cytometry. Meanwhile, same experiment has been carried on Fe-C QDs@Poly B to verify the •OH production ability of Fe-C QDs@Poly B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing the LPS with colorimetry method
ce:section.ce:para: Before the analysis, the importance of Poly B had been verified with the comparison of Fe-C QDs@Poly B and Fe-C QDs after reaction with 1 EU/mL LPS for 10 min. The formal analysis of LPS via colorimetric method was as followed: firstly,50 μL 20 mM TMB and 20 μL 0.05 g/mL Fe-C QDs@Poly B was added in 100 mM pH 4.0 acetate buffer, secondly, different concentrations of LPS were added in the solvent, the concentrations of LPS were 0.1–5 EU/mL, at last, 1 mM H2O2 was added. The solution was reacted under room temperature for 10 min, then the absorbance at 652 nm was tested by microplate reader. Furthermore, control experiment was carried out to study the selectivity of the as-proposed analytical method. Therein, hyaluronic acid, β-renieratene, arachidic acid, jasmonic acid, phosphatidycholine, and chitosan were chosen as the interferences, which were all at the concentration of 10-time LPS one. The absorbance data have been recorded separately under the same condition for LPS measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing the LPS with fluorescence platform
ce:section.ce:para: For precisely quantification of LPS, micro cuvette was utilized for fluorescence sensing of LPS. 180 μL Fe-C QDs@Poly B (50 mg/mL) and different concentration of LPS (0.0025–10 EU/mL) were mixed in 400 μL micro cuvette and reacted for 10 min. Then the fluorescence intensity of mixed solution was measured and recorded on fluorescence spectrometer. Subsequently, 250 ng/mL Hyaluronic acid, β-renieratene, arachidic acid, jasmonic acid, phosphatidycholine, chitosan was mixed with the Fe-C QDs@Poly B and reacted at the same condition to record the fluorescence intensity for selective measurement of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B with 1 EU/mL LPS as comparison. (Scheme 1 )

The Fe-C QDs was prepared via one step thermos solvent method based on the precursors of FeCl2·4H2O and critic acid. The morphology of the Fe-C QDs had been characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as depicted in Fig. 1
a, the prepared Fe-C QDs performed well-dispersed and average-sized spherical morphology, the average size of Fe-C QDs is 3.27 (0.114) nm (Fig. S1). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) results showed that the Fe-C QDs exhibited highly parallel and well-ordered crystal lattices, the lattices spacing of 0.180, 0.252, 0.312 nm were observed in the crystal structure of Fig. 1b, which is consistent with the diffraction planes of (420) and (620) of Fe2O3 (JCPDS 16–0653) and (018) of Fe2O3 (JCPDS 33–0664) [22,23]. XRD results also confirmed the Fe-C QDs equipped similar crystalized structure of Fe2O3. The main peak around 2θ = 33.152◦ was ascribed to the (104) face of Fe2O3 (JCPDS 33–0664) besides, 2θ = 12.39◦ and 23.271◦ were attributed to (111) and (311) face of C60 (JCPDS no.49–1717), respectively [23–25], all the results are confirmed with the HRTEM results (Fig. 1b and c). The carboxyl groups were detrimental for Fe-C QDs, which determines the modification of Polymyxin B for LPS recognition. The FTIR result showed that the peaks in the regions of 3010-3500 and 1729 cm−1 were attributed to –OH and CO [26,27] (Fig. 1d) which indicated the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface. High resolution XPS was performed to precisely analysis the element composition of prepared Fe-C QDs, 3 major elements peaks belonging to Fe, C and O at 709.89, 384.76, and 532.14 eV had been observed in Fig. 3 a [28,29], and their composition ratio was Fe: 0.98 %, O: 44.41 % and C: 52.71 %. The O 1S result was in consistent with the FTIR spectra to confirm the existence of carboxyl group. 2 main summits situated in 531.65 eV and 532.61 eV had been founded in the O 1S spectra, which are corresponding to CO and OH, respectively. Apart from these, the C 1S analysis result indicated that 4 main summits: CFe at 283.9 eV; CC at 284.8 eV; CO at 286.7 eV and CO at 288.7 eV (Fig. 2c) [30–32]. In the Fe 2P spectra, 2 major summits are belonging to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 has been analysed and these results demonstrated that the subsistence of Fe (II) and Fe (III) [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the Fe-C QDs@Poly B
ce:section.ce:para: Polymyxin B (Poly B) is a easily obtained specific recognition component with higher specificity for LPS and more stable stability compared with other ligands [34,35], more importantly, the toxicity of LPS could be effectively reduced by the positive charge of Poly B [36] ,. The previous work indicated that the Poly B could absorbed to the outer membrane of the gram-negative bacterium and exhibited special affinity to lipid A of the LPS [37,38]. Besides, the abundant amino groups on the Poly B’s surface made it easily for modification [39]. As the recognition ligand of LPS, the successful modification of Poly B on the Fe-C QDs are vital for quantification of LPS. As shown in Fig. 3a, zeta potential of Fe-C QDs@Poly B was less negative compared with bare Fe-C QDs due to the neutralization of positive charge of Poly B. Hydrodynamic diameter changes during the coagulation also confirmed the successful modification, as illustrated in Fig. 3b, a significant diameter increasement was observed after Poly B conjugation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis further confirmed the successful modification of Poly B. EIS measurements were performed to characterize the electron transfer capacities of the modified electrodes with pure Nafion/Fe-C QDs and Nafion/Fe-C QDs@Poly B, respectively. As described in Fig. 3c, the bare Fe-C QDs exhibited a small semicircular diameter (curve a), which indicates that low electron-transfer resistance (Ret) for the redox pair, i.e. [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−. After modification with Fe-C QDs@Poly B, the resulted electrode showed much bigger semicircle diameter compared with the pure Fe-C QDs modified one.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characterization of Fe-C QDs and Fe-C QDs@Poly B
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence properties of Fe-C QDs and Fe-C QDs@Poly B were recorded on spectrofluorometer as depicted in Fig. 4 a, the optimist excitation and emission wavelength of Fe-C QDs are 352 nm and 470 nm respectively. During the 320 nm to 390 nm the emission summits were gradually increased with the increasement of excitation wavelength, indicated the emission spectra was qualified with dependence of excitation, as Fig. 4b depicted. In Fig. 4c, the 1931 CIE chromatically diagram of Fe-C QDs showed 3 classic points belonging to blue fluorescence emission, A (0.1604, 0.1502), B (0.1634, 0.1661), C (0.1683, 0.2525) were respectively corresponding to the excitation wavelengths at 320 nm, 340 nm and 390 nm. The color of Fe-C QDs under different concentration at 365 nm was diverse as shown in Fig. S1. After that, the fluorescence property of Fe-C QDs@Poly B was also investigated, compared with the non-Poly B modified Fe-C QDs, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-C QDs@Poly B was clearly decreased due to the enlarged and unevenly distributed size, while the best wavelengths of excitation and emission were red-shifted to 400 nm and 494 nm (Fig. 4d) due to the increasement of carboxyl group from Poly B [40]. Apart from this, the size of Fe-C QDs@Poly B was dominantly increased due to the Poly B modification, which further caused the emission spectra’s quality that dependence of excitation was obviously enhanced (Fig. 4e). The 1931 CIE chromatically diagram also exhibited the sightly influence on fluorescent emission of Poly B modification, in Fig. 4f, the A (0.1668, 0.1969), B (0.1695, 0.2574) and C (0.1900, 0.3576) were apart corresponded to the excitation wavelengths at 370 nm, 400 nm and 430 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-C QDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectrophotometry was performed in the substrates of H2O2 and TMB to investigate the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-C QDs. With the presence of H2O2, Fe-C QDs could catalyze TMB to its oxidation with the characteristic absorbance peak at 652 nm, while bare H2O2 could not catalyze TMB (Fig. 5 A). These results above demonstrated that Fe-C QDs equipped with excellent peroxidase-like activity. The usage of FeCl2·4H2O was then optimized, and 0.1 g FeCl2·4H2O performed best peroxidase activity (Fig. S3). To verify whether the peroxidase-like activity changed after Poly B modification, same experiment had been performed on Fe-C QDs@Poly B. As Fig. 5b illustrated, the peroxidase-like activity was slightly decreased after Poly B modification. The usage of Poly B had important influence on the size and property of sensor, the usage was also optimized to 1 mg with the optimist peroxidase activity for LPS analysis (Fig. S4). Subsequently, detection of •OH was further conducted via the terephthalic acid (TA) based fluorescent assay. As depicted in Fig. 5c, a clear fluorescence enhancement was observed after the addition of Fe-C QDs, reflecting the •OH generated by Fe-C QDs. After the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity increased much more significantly, indicating that the Fe-C QDs could efficiently convert H2O2 to •OH. Accordingly, the phenomenon was indicative of the POD-like activity of the produced Fe-C QDs. Similarly, the •OH generation property of Fe-C QDs@Poly B was measured under the same condition, as Fig. 5d depicted, the addition of Fe-C QDs@Poly B would intrigue enhancement of fluorescence intensity, while the intensity is slightly lower than Fe-C QDs under same concentration, which is consistent with the result of peroxidase-like activity test. After that, to achieve the best peroxidase-like activity of Fe-C QDs@Poly B, the reaction conditions such as temperature and pH had been explored. Results indicated that the optimist temperature and pH of Fe-C QDs@Poly B were 70 °C and 4.0, (Fig. 5e and f).

In order to further study the POD-like activity of Fe-C QDs, steady-state kinetic analysis was carried out at pH of 4.0 under room temperature. The Michaelis-Menten curves were plotted for Fe-C QDs and1mM H2O2 with different TMB concentrations as shown in Fig. 6 a. Subsequently the Michaelis-Menten curves were plotted at 2 mM TMB with different concentration of H2O2 in Fig. 6b. the Vmax and Km were measured by the Lineweaver-Burk model (Fig. 6c and 6d). Moreover, the POD-like activity kinetic parameters of Fe-C QDs were compared with other POD-like materials. As the Table S1 illustrated, the prepared Fe-C QDs has larger Vmax and low Km, this indicated that the Fe-C QDs was equipped with excellent POD-like activity and substrate affinity. Meanwhile, the comparison with other materials based on iron and carbon also confirmed the excellent peroxidase activity of Fe-C QDs (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of the LPS by dual channels system
ce:section.ce:para: The colormetric and fluorescence system have been constructed to explore the sensing ability of prepared Fe-C QDs@Poly B. The importance of Poly B had been verified with the comparison of Fe-C QDs@Poly B and Fe-C QDs, which indicated that the signal output by Fe-C QDs@Poly B are 20 times higher than Fe-C QDs (Fig. S5). The colormetric test of LPS by Fe-C QDs@Poly B was under room temperature at pH 4.0. With the increasement of concentration of LPS, the blue colour of solution gets dark, and the absorbance data at 652 nm was gradually increased (Fig. 7 a, Fig. S6a). The regression formula Y = 0.184X + 0.112 (R2 = 0.999) was calculated by concentration of LPS and the data of Δ absorbance data ((A-A0)/A0) where A0 and A are the fluorescence intensities reacted without or with target respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.09 EU/mL with the equation [41] of LODn = 3.3 (SENn -2σx 2 + h0SENn -2 σx 2 + h0σycal 2)1/2 and limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated as 0.297 EU/mL by LOQn = 10 (SENn -2σx 2 + h0SENn -2 σx 2 + h0σycal 2)1/2. Hyaluronic acid, β-renieratene, arachidic acid, jasmonic acid, phosphatidycholine, chitosan, were reacted with the Fe-C QDs@Poly B to verify the selectivity of colorimetric detection system, as Fig. 7c illustrated, Δ absorbance was clearly changed after reaction with LPS compared with others, meanwhile, it could be clearly distinguished even mixed with the distractors (Fig. S6b). This indicated the constructed Fe-C QDs@Poly B could be utilized in colorimetric assay of LPS. The sensitivity and analytical sensitivity of the colorimetric chanel was analysed using the following equations: SENn = σx/σy and γn = SENn/σx [41],respectively, exhibiting high sensitivity of 55.18 and analytical sensitivity of 9.09.

Although, rapid and visible LPS analysis had been fulfilled by the colorimetric sensing platform, the lack of high sensitivity still haunted. Based on the dual channels sensing platform has been constructed via the combination of colorimetric and fluorescence, which would exhibit high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. As illustrated in Fig. 7d the fluorescence intensity of Fe-C QDs@Poly B was gradually quenched with the increasement of LPS concentration. Besides, an outstanding linear ship had been observed during the concentration region from 0.0025 to 10 EU/mL (Fig. 7e), the regression equation was Y = 0.07X + 0.556 (R2 = 0.998). The LOD was calculated to be 0.0023 EU/mL with the equation and LOQ was calculated as 0.0076 EU/mL, the LOD was improved 39 times compared with the colorimetric method. Subsequently, the selectivity measurement was carried on the fluorescence system of Fe-C QDs@Poly B as depicted in Fig. 7f, the Δ fluorescence intensity reacted with LPS was decreased extraordinarily compared with others’, furthermore, even in complicated environment that mixed with other distractors would not interrupt the analysis of LPS. Thus, the combination between colorimetric and fluorescence platform would enhance the sensitivity of LPS analysis and maintain high selectivity meanwhile.

Herein, the principle of dual channel platform for LPS detection based on Fe-C QDs@Poly B can be supposed as follows. Generally, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dots was dependent on the surface functionalization, size distribution. Upon the recognition of LPS, the Poly B would conjugate with LPS thus enlarging the size of Fe-C QDs @ Poly B-LPS. Meanwhile, the size was also distributed relatively unevenly as indicated in s, Thus, the unevenly and ampliative size (Fig. S7) downgrade the fluorescent intensity of the Fe-C QDs @ Poly B-LPS accordingly, at the same time, the fluorescence spectrum indicated that the emission spectrum of LPS was partly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of the Fe-C QDs @ Poly B, the optimum excitation summit of Fe-C QDs @ Poly B was also close to the emission summit of LPS (Fig. S8). Meanwhile, the peroxidase activity center of Fe-C QDS was disclosed upon the conjunction, hence, the POD–like activity was dramatically increased to endow the unparalleled selectivity (Fig. 8 ). The fluorescence lifetimes of Fe-C QDs; Fe-C QDs @ Poly B; Fe-C QDs @ Poly B-LPS also confirmed our proposed mechanism as illustrated in Fig. S9a–c. The lifetime of bare Fe-C QDs was 0.66 ns, after modification of Poly B, the fluorescence lifetime was decreased to 0.62 ns, after recognition and conjugation of LPS, the lifetime decreased to 0.61 ns. The results above indicated that the modification of Poly B and conjugation of LPS would decrease the fluorescence lifetime of Fe-C QDs, which in consistent with the result of downgrade of fluorescence intensity in the fluorescence system.

Artificial serum spiked experiment had been performed to verify the practicability of constructed colorimetry and fluorescence dual channels LPS analytical system. 3 samples of artificial serum had been spiked with 0.005–1 EU/mL, subsequently, fluorescence and colorimetry systems were performed on the samples respectively. The recovery rate was 97 %–106 % and RSD was less than 8.5 % (Table S2). For broader application, 3 % NaCl, 2.5 % glucose, 0.1 % critic acid, 0.25 % sodium bicarbonate salt solution were used to simulation of complex environment, and the RSD was less than 13 % with 94–105 % recovery rate. These further confirmed the practicality and feasibility of dual channels sensing platform (Table S4). Compared with the prevalent analytical method, our constructed dual channels exhibited more precise sensitivity and wider linear range as concluded in Table 1 . Simultaneously, the valid period was also explored, as depicted in Fig. S10, 3 samples under different concentration all exhibited excellent stability after 1 week, confirmed a long-term durability for practical application. Subsequently, a series of water samples had been analyzed with the dual channels platform and the result was compared with the result of LAL kit. As list in Table 2 , the detection results of LPS in the real samples are in good agreement with the LAL method results and the accuracies are close to 100 %, indicated the potential of dual channels platform for commercial use as succedaneum of LAL method. To explore the application in living cells analysis, the dual channels platform was used for Escherichia Coli sample detection. A good linear range from 2.5 × 105 to 1.5 × 107 was observed with the regression equation Y = 0.46X + 0.105 (R2 = 0.991) (Fig. S11a). The selectivity analysis via colorimetry method also performed convincible discrimination with Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Acinetobacter baumannii as comparison (Fig. S11b and c).

In summary, a dual channels sensing platform based on Fe-C QDS@ Poly B had been successfully constructed and exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity in LPS analysis. The colorimetry sensing system based on POD-like activity provided outstanding selectivity in visible analysis of LPS, and the prominent fluorescence property make sure the sensing of LPS could be utilized on trace level. The complemented colorimetry and fluorescence sensing system make the short ends up and performed a highly efficient platform for LPS analysis. The LOD was estimated to 0.0023 EU /mL and the linear range of LPS sensing based on the fluorescence system is 0.0025–10 EU/mL. The artificial spiked experiment on serum also provided a strongly back up for practicality in real sample analysis. Thus, the construction of the dual channel platform for LPS analysis has provided a new path to draw aspiration for futural LPS detection analysis.

Limin Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Cai Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shuo Shan: Data curation. Yifan Zhang: Software. Shengnan Chen: Data curation. Tianming Wu: Data curation. Yusheng Niu: Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Yuanhong Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22274084), Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2021JQ04), Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (No. tsqn201909088), Outstanding Youth Innovation Team of Universities in Shandong Province (2019KJA027).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151692.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free bacterial toxin detection in water supplies using porous silicon nanochannel sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Reta, A. Michelmore, C.P. Saint, B. Prieto-Simon, N.H. Voelcker, Label-Free Bacterial Toxin Detection in Water Supplies Using Porous Silicon Nanochannel Sensors, ACS Sensors 4(6) (2019) 1515-1523. https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.8b01670.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy-based biosensor for the detection of biological macromolecules: the case of the lipopolysaccharide endotoxin molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Rusciano, A. Capaccio, A. Sasso, A. Capo, C.M. Almuzara, M. Staiano, S. D’Auria, A. Varriale, A Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy-Based Biosensor for the Detection of Biological Macromolecules: The Case of the Lipopolysaccharide Endotoxin Molecules, Int. J. Mol. Sci., 2023.
